original_hypothesis.title: Pagination Bug in `getPositionsForCurator` Causes Reverts and Data Corruption
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `getPositionsForCurator` function uses the loop variable `i` (which starts at `start_`) to index into the newly allocated `credIds` and `amounts` arrays (which are sized `stopIndex - start_`). If `start_ > 0`, `i` will exceed the bounds of the arrays, causing a revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Incorrect array indexing in view functions breaks integrations and frontends that rely on pagination to read user state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credIds[i] = credId; amounts[i] = amount; where i can be >= credIds.length

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using global loop index for a locally sized array
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The array is sized `stopIndex - start_`, but accessed at index `i` which starts at `start_`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use the `index` variable for array assignment

analysis_questions: Does the function revert for all paginated calls?
Does the assembly truncation at the end discard valid data when `start_ == 0` but entries are skipped?
validation_requirements: Verify the call reverts with an out-of-bounds error

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A frontend or external smart contract attempts to paginate a user's large portfolio of credentials by calling `getPositionsForCurator(user, 10, 20)`. The call reverts due to an array out-of-bounds error, breaking the integration and preventing the user from viewing or interacting with their portfolio.
prerequisites: User has enough positions to require pagination

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but breaks core protocol functionality

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pagination with start_ > 0

validation_result.impact_summary: getPositionsForCurator reverts for any call with start_ > 0 due to array out-of-bounds access, and produces incorrect sparse data even when start_ == 0 if entries are skipped.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The getPositionsForCurator function allocates result arrays credIds and amounts with size (stopIndex - start_), meaning valid indices are 0 through (stopIndex - start_ - 1). However, the loop variable i starts at start_ and is used directly to index into these arrays (credIds[i] and amounts[i]). When start_ > 0, the first loop iteration attempts credIds[start_] which exceeds the array bounds, causing an immediate revert. For example, calling getPositionsForCurator(curator, 10, 20) allocates arrays of size 10 (indices 0-9) but the loop starts at i=10, causing an out-of-bounds access on the very first iteration. The correct fix is to use credIds[index] and amounts[index] instead of credIds[i] and amounts[i].
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at Cred.sol lines 343-344 is confirmed. The code uses credIds[i] and amounts[i] where i starts at start_, but the arrays are sized (stopIndex - start_). This is a clear indexing bug that causes reverts when start_ > 0.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Arrays credIds and amounts are allocated with size (stopIndex - start_), giving valid indices 0 to (stopIndex - start_ - 1). The loop variable i starts at start_, so credIds[i] accesses index start_ which is out-of-bounds when start_ > 0. Even when start_ == 0, using i instead of index produces sparse arrays with gaps when entries are filtered out, and the subsequent assembly truncation discards trailing valid data.

calling_sequence: 2. stopIndex is set to 20 (or userCredIds.length if smaller)
3. start_ (10) < stopIndex (20), so no revert at pagination check
4. credIds = new uint256[](20 - 10) => array of size 10 (indices 0-9)
5. amounts = new uint256[](20 - 10) => array of size 10 (indices 0-9)
7. First iteration: credIds[10] = credId => OUT OF BOUNDS REVERT (array size is 10, max index is 9)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The function allocates arrays of size (stopIndex - start_) but indexes them with loop variable i which ranges from start_ to stopIndex-1. When start_ > 0, i starts at a value >= the array length, causing an immediate out-of-bounds revert. The fix should use credIds[index] and amounts[index] where index is the local counter starting at 0.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Not config dependent. This is a pure code logic bug in a view function.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Any caller (frontend, external contract, or direct user) attempting paginated reads with start_ > 0 will experience a revert. This is guaranteed for any deployment since it is a pure logic error in array indexing. A user with many cred positions (e.g., 50+) who needs paginated retrieval cannot use this function for any page beyond the first.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. This is a view function bug that breaks pagination functionality. While it does not directly cause financial loss, it breaks core protocol functionality for reading user positions. Frontends and integrating contracts relying on pagination will fail. Users with many positions cannot retrieve their full portfolio data through this function. The bug requires a contract upgrade to fix.
recommended_mitigations: Replace credIds[i] with credIds[index] and amounts[i] with amounts[index] in the loop body
Add comprehensive unit tests for getPositionsForCurator with start_ > 0 parameters
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Cred contract. 2. Create multiple creds and buy shares for a test curator address so they have >10 positions. 3. Call getPositionsForCurator(curator, 10, 20). 4. Observe revert due to array out-of-bounds. 5. Also test getPositionsForCurator(curator, 0, 0) with some _credIdExistsPerAddress entries set to false to observe sparse array + incorrect truncation behavior.
additional_testing_needed: Test behavior when start_ == 0 but some _credIdExistsPerAddress entries are false (sparse array + truncation issue)
Verify whether any on-chain contracts depend on this function (could cause cascading failures)
